data.title: flyExclusive: Cost Management Is Taking Shape, Not A Buy Yet
data.description:
flyExclusive (NYSE:FLYX), a company providing private travel solutions, is one of the few stocks on which I have a sell rating. Since my last report published in August 2024, the stock has lost 19% compared to the S&P 500’s 14.8% gain. Moreover, since I initiated coverage of the stock has lost over 44% of its value. The stock rose sharply following its Q2 2025 earnings report. In this article, I assess whether the stock offers a more compelling entry point at current price levels.

flyExclusive Revenues Increase, Losses Taper

Second quarter revenues increased 15.6% to $91.3 million but missed analyst expectations of $92 million in sales. The stock price, however, increased significantly, and I can understand why.

The previous times I analyzed flyExclusive, the company had some non-performing jets, and it planned on replacing those aircraft to drive financial performance. Over the past 12 months, the company has removed 24 out of the 37 non-performing jets. That means that loss-making jets are replaced by aircraft that do generate positive margins. The second quarter earnings show the financial progress made with top-line growth exceeding cost of revenues. Total operating expenses increased by a bit less than $3 million, but combined with the top line growth, the operating loss tapered from $21.8 million to $12.4 million.

flyExclusive derived revenues from four business lines. The first one is Jet Club and Charter, which essentially allows members to book flights at a fixed rate formula paid on top of their membership. That line of business saw revenue grow 9.6% to $79.5 million. The second line of business is fractional ownership, which saw nearly a doubling in revenues to $8.5 million. Maintenance activities grew 28% to $2.9 million, and aircraft management services is the fourth and newest line of business. Essentially, what we saw in the second quarter was an improvement in utilization, active member growth, and higher members per aircraft.

Operating cash burn improved from $42.2 million to $10.1 million, while capital expenditures decreased from $28.1 million to $4.1 million. This led to a free cash flow of negative $14.2 million compared to $70.3 million a year ago. The company had proceeds of $31.5 million compared to $23.7 million a year ago, leading to a free cash flow, including proceeds from sales of property and equipment, of positive $17.3 million compared to a burn of $46.6 million a year ago.

flyExclusive Stock Price Valuation

flyExclusive is not profitable on the EBITDA level. For that reason, using the EV/EBITDA valuation method, which is my preferred method to value stocks, is not suitable. Instead, I am using an EV/Sales valuation method. A complicating factor is that there are not that many peers for flyExclusive to establish typical EV/sales multiple. I am using a 1-2x multiple for established operators, 1.5x-3x for fast-growing operators with a membership component, and >2x for asset light operators. flyExclusive does not match either of these three categories exactly, but I do believe that a 2x EV/sales valuation would be fair.

Based on roughly 10% annual sales growth and a total of 80.1 million class A and class B shares, I would get to a stock price of $3.57 for this year, indicating a fair valuation and 10% and 22% upside for the years ahead. However, given that the company remains loss-making and I typically look for mature companies to show around 10% upside, I don’t believe the upside that the stock price evaluation returns provide an extremely compelling investment case for flyExclusive, as the risk profile (continued cash burns and operating losses) would command a significantly higher upside to flip from sell to buy. As a result, I am upgrading from sell to hold and note that while I assumed that the company will cover cash shortfalls through debt, issuing shares is also an option as the company has filed a Form S-3 indicating it may sell common stock, preferred stock, debt securities, and other rights to raise up to $250 million.

Conclusion: flyExclusive Cost Rationalization Gains Traction

flyExclusive is significantly cutting costs as the company is transitioning to a fleet that is better able to generate profits. However, even to hold the stock, I believe there are substantial risks as the company remains loss making and I believe turning profitable is a multi-year effort that may lead to shareholder dilution. So, while an EV/Sales assessment at a 2x multiple indicates upside, it is definitely not enough to justify a buy rating.

data.title: Navigating Thematic Investing With THRO
data.description:
By Jay Jacobs

Why Themes Matter

Thematic investing aims to capture long-term structural changes like artificial intelligence (AI) and geopolitical fragmentation, as well as shorter-lived shifts such as the rise of the cost-conscious consumer. Some themes reshape industries over decades, while others make a sharp but brief impact. In both scenarios, timing matters. Knowing when to enter, lean in, or step back can be the difference between capturing a trend or chasing it. (For a deeper look into AI and geopolitical fragmentation, see our Thematic Mid-Year Update.)

THRO: A data-driven thematic approach

One way to access thematic investing is through the iShares U.S. Thematic Rotation Active ETF (THRO). It takes an adaptive, data-driven approach, analyzing millions of news articles, earnings call transcripts, and analyst reports in an effort to pinpoint trends gaining traction with investors and markets. Unlike strategies tied to a single theme, THRO evaluates dozens of themes, aiming to outperform the S&P 500.

Sample data sources

1,000,000+

Financial news articles per year

5,000+

Earrings call transcripts every quarter

1,000+

Broker baskets and thematic ETFs

Source: BlackRock, as of December 31, 2024. The strategies discussed are strictly for illustrative and educational purposes and are not a recommendation, offer, or solicitation to buy or sell any securities or to adopt any investment strategy. There is no guarantee that any strategies discussed will be effective.

Proprietary signals can help guide portfolio positioning. Themes showing strong investor interest, positive price momentum, and attractive valuations may warrant a larger allocation, while those losing relevance or overheating can be dialed down or removed altogether. This dynamic approach can be nimble, rotating into high-conviction opportunities while staying disciplined on risk.

THRO In Action

To see how this process works in action, we look at three of THRO’s current positions: two overweights and one underweight.

TJX Companies (TJX): Tightening the Belt: Down the Consumer Value Chain

With economic uncertainty persisting, consumers are reprioritizing how they spend. A recent McKinsey survey found that most Americans have either adjusted their shopping habits or plan to do so by cutting back or switching to lower-cost brands.1 Recognizing this potential trend, THRO has an overweight exposure to the Tightening the Belt theme.

Not all retailers are equally positioned to benefit. Companies with flexible sourcing models may have an edge over those more dependent on imports. As a leader in off-price retail, TJX Companies (1.4% in THRO)2 sells brand-name goods, often 20%-60% lower than those found in traditional retail, by purchasing excess inventory already located in the U.S.3 This model helps it sidestep supply chain shocks while potentially capitalizing on softening demand in full-price retail.

The Tightening the Belt theme has been a contributor to THRO’s performance, outpacing both Consumer Discretionary and Consumer Staples sectors through May 2025. THRO’s consumer basket reflects an overweight stance relative to the S&P 500’s consumer sectors, tapping into the shift toward value-conscious spending and showing how THRO seeks to turn real-world consumer behavior into investment opportunities.

2025 performance of THR0 thematic consumer exposure overweight vs. consumer sectors of S&P 500 Index

Cisco Systems (CSCO): Capex continued: Tech & infrastructure

Major technology companies are aggressively ramping up capital expenditures, with industry giants expected to spend $305 billion in 2025, largely to advance their AI ambitions.4 These investments extend well beyond chips and servers, flowing into fiber optics, power and cooling systems, and electronic components. THRO seeks to participate in this digital buildout through its overweight in the Capex Continued: Tech & Infrastructure theme.

Cisco Systems (1.3% in THRO)5, a provider of internet networking products and solutions, plays a role in enabling this expansion. As a potential to beneficiary of AI, automation, and reshoring tailwinds, Cisco received $600 million in AI product orders in 3Q25, surpassing its $1 billion full year target a quarter early. Demand from hyperscale cloud providers was a driver, with some accounts increasing orders by more than 100%.6 THRO’s position in Cisco shows how the fund seeks exposure across the broader ecosystem that may go beyond the more obvious names associated with AI.

JPMorgan Chase (JPM): Globally Integrated Service Providers

The impact of regulatory policies and geopolitical tensions has extended beyond supply chains. They’re slowing global capital flows and cross-border deal activity. Investment banking fees fell 6.3% year-over-year in early 2025,7 and a PwC survey found that 30% of deals were paused or revised due to shifting trade policies.8 Even approved deals risk being rerouted to regional players as firms seek more predictable operating environments. Since many U.S. megabanks already have significant domestic exposure, global diversification may be a potential growth driver. In the meantime, the slowdown of global trade could accelerate financial reforms in other regions and reduce the need for U.S.-based services.

JPMorgan Chase (0% in THRO)9, a bellwether for multinational banking and financial services, faces these pressures given its position as a global investment bank. On its 1Q25 earnings call, CEO Jamie Dimon noted that large U.S. banks could be “in the crosshairs” of prolonged trade disputes. JPM’s vast network, moving over $10 trillion daily across 160+ countries in 120 currencies, shows how exposed global players may be to market fragmentation.10 These dynamics support THRO’s underweight position in both Globally Integrated Service Providers theme and JPM.

Conclusion

THRO’s active approach is designed for agility, adjusting exposures as market conditions evolve, leaning into areas of strength, and stepping back from those losing momentum. This discipline seeks to maintain the portfolio aligned with investor priorities while pursuing new opportunities for growth and return.

Holdings as of 7/31/2025 and subject to change. There is no guarantee that the companies discussed remain in the fund. For a complete list of most recent holdings, click on the fund card below. This is not meant as a guarantee of any future result or experience. 4 BlackRock, “Thematic investing mid-year update,” August 2025. Forward-looking estimates may not come to pass.

5 BlackRock as of 7/31/2025.

data.title: Merck: Buy The Weakness
data.description:
Merck (NYSE:MRK) has substantially underperformed the market over the past few years. The company faces the upcoming loss of exclusivity of key drug Keytruda along with a number of expensive acquisitions, such as the recent $10 billion Verona Pharma acquisition. Despite that, the company's financials remain strong as it works to diversify itself and generate strong returns.

Merck Financial Results

Merck had reasonable financial results as it's managed a difficult market.

The company saw a 2% decline in sales but a 1.3% increase in non-GAAP gross margin. That enabled the company's operating earnings to actually go up. However, the company saw a 7% increase in non-GAAP operating expenses along with an increase in tax rates. As a result, the company saw a 5% decline in non-GAAP EPS.

That non-GAAP EPS puts the company at a single-digit P/E indicating its financial strength.

Merck Pipeline

The company has an impressive pipeline with strong drugs in each category.

In Oncology, the company has continued to achieve new indications from Keytruda, while continuing to present data to expand its Oncology portfolio. In infectious diseases, the company is continuing to expand its vaccine treatment, incredibly important vaccines that save lives.

The company's vaccine pipeline is expected to generate more than $5 billion in 2030 sales.

Merck + Verona

The company has announced the acquisition of Verona.

The main target here is "Ohtuvayre" the first novel inhaled COPD maintenance treatment in >20 years. The drug has already been approved, substantially reducing the risk of drug approval that comes with many acquisitions. The drug has had a slow initial start, but peak sales are still forecast to be in the $3-4 billion range.

That'd be very profitable for a $10 billion acquisition.

Merck - Keytruda

The company's Keytruda sales have continued to increase substantially as it finds more and more approval.

The drug continues to be accepted for more and more forms of cancer, with increased updates and support from different indications. Even with the success of Ozempic, the drug has maintained its slot as the best-selling drug, with more than $30 billion in forecast 2025 sales.

The company is working to handle an expected loss of sales, though we expect growing sales mean the drug will remain stronger for longer. The company has tripled its late-phase pipeline since 2021, and sees >$50 billion in mid-2030s opportunity from its pipeline. That's more than enough to make up for the losses from Keytruda.

Merck Shareholder Returns

Overall, the company has updated its guidance on strength, which will support shareholder returns.

The company has bumped revenue guidance by $200 million with flat margins and a $100 million decline at the midpoint in operating expenses. The company also expects average taxes to come down 0.25% at the midpoint. The net result here is that the company's EPS guidance has shifted to be a few cents higher at the midpoint.

That just barely ekes the company into a single digit P/E based on its non-GAAP EPS showing its low valuation.

Those strong earnings give the company substantial cash for shareholder returns despite continued R&D and capital spending. The company's $2.1 billion quarterly dividend is an almost 3.9% dividend yield it can comfortably afford. The company also repurchased $1.3 billion shares in the quarter, ~0.5% of its float.

The company has a $10 billion authorization, enough to repurchase 5% of its outstanding shares. Over the past 12 months, the company has spent $8 billion on dividends and $3.4 billion on share repurchases, both of which it can comfortably afford. As Keytruda hits peak sales until LOE in 3 years, and new drugs come online, immediate-term returns should be strong.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is the uncertain nature of the pharmaceutical industry. The company has a major loss of exclusivities coming up, and it will need to find a way to replace $10s of billions of revenue. The company has an exciting pipeline for that, but there's no guarantee it works as expected, which is worth paying close attention.

Conclusion

Merck's share price has continued to decline, moving it into a single-digit P/E based on its non-GAAP EPS. The company has slightly increased its 2025 revenue guidance and decreased its expense guidance as Keytruda has continued to perform incredibly well. That has enabled the company to maintain its strong dividend and share buybacks.

The company does have the risk of loss of exclusivity, but it has an impressive pipeline. The company's pipeline will start panning out with acquisitions such as Verona and new vaccine approvals. We expect that the company will be able to replace the loss of exclusivity and generate continued long-term shareholder returns.

data.title: High Yield: When Boring Is Better
data.description:
By Gershon M. Distenfeld, CFA & Will Smith, CFA

Elevated yields and conservative balance sheets are helping high yield stay resilient amid trade uncertainty.

While equity markets have run hot and cold, high-yield bonds have been steady performers over the past 12 months. Following a strong second quarter, high yield is one of the top-performing fixed-income asset classes thus far in 2025. We expect this momentum to continue on the strength of sound fundamentals, elevated yields and conservative balance sheet management.

On the heels of additional tariff announcements and bellicose trade rhetoric, investors are now digesting weaker-than-expected jobs growth and evidence of economic slowing. You might expect these economic risks to rattle the high-yield market. But they haven’t and, in our view, likely won’t. Here’s why.

Fundamentals Support Tight Credit Spreads

Corporate fundamentals remain sound despite recent softening, both in the US and Europe. Yes, credit downgrades are outpacing upgrades, while interest coverage ratios and EBITDA margins have deteriorated. But fundamentals are coming off a position of remarkable strength following a post-pandemic housecleaning that cleared out underperformers and increased overall credit quality. These metrics are now inching back toward their long-term averages (Display).

High-Yield Fundamentals Have Weakened

As for today’s narrow credit spreads, we’re not especially concerned. Given elevated rates, spreads comprise a smaller portion of yield. More importantly, spreads don’t drive returns; yield does. While a bond’s spread reflects in-the-moment assessments of its credit risk relative to Treasuries, yield to worst captures the bond’s expected return from both government yield levels and credit spreads.

That’s why, in our analysis, yield to worst is a reliable proxy for five-year forward returns, regardless of the environment. Yield to worst is currently hovering near the top 25% of its 10-year range (Display). Given these elevated yields, we expect high-yield returns to compete with those of stocks in the coming years.

Yields Are Near 10-Year Highs

July 2015–June 2025

Of course, there’s a risk that spreads could widen. We saw this in April when credit spreads blew out temporarily in reaction to new tariffs. But the sector was resilient, and spreads eventually retreated. This highlights both the sticky nature of spreads, which can stay range-bound for extended periods, and the solid fundamentals of today’s high-yield market.

That said, we think spreads may widen as short rates rally on Fed rate-cut expectations. But because high-yield bonds would likely benefit from the rate rally, high-yield bond prices could rise even as spreads widen. Lastly, trade-related headline risks aren’t going away soon, and we anticipate continued spread volatility accordingly.

We expect yields to provide ample cushion against the negative price effects of spread widening, underscoring the high-yield market’s resilience.

Trade Uncertainty has Led to Corporate Belt Tightening

Beyond upending the financial markets and keeping US trading partners guessing, trade uncertainty has had the unintended effect of strengthening corporate balance sheets. Because it’s difficult to budget against a chaotic backdrop, high-yield issuers have chosen instead to bolster working capital and keep debt levels manageable as they wait to see how the tariff and trade picture settles.

This more conservative fiscal approach is reflected in both the limited volume of net new issuance in the European and US markets and issuers’ use of proceeds. An explosion of new issuance would be concerning—both to us and to the markets—particularly if it were used to finance leveraged buyouts and M&A activity. But that’s not what we’re seeing.

Instead, new issuance has largely involved rolling over debt and refinancing (Display)—benign corporate actions that have kept spreads sticky and the high-yield universe relatively clean. Even in the event of an external shock, we wouldn’t expect the high-yield markets to buckle, because market excesses have largely been wrung from the system.

Recent High-Yield Issuance Largely Comprises Refinancing

Issuance: Use of Proceeds (Percent)

Trade wars have not only forced issuers to manage debt more carefully but have also assuaged any lingering concerns about a looming maturity wall. Maturity walls have worried investors for the past 30 years, as there frequently appears to be a large amount of debt due to mature in the next two to four years. This time, with issuers quietly refinancing and near-term maturities skewing toward higher-quality debt, what could have been a concern has largely passed uneventfully—a trend we expect to continue.

High-yield bonds are by no means immune to economic disruption—nor are they recession-proof. But with credit quality high, yields elevated and issuers managing their balance sheets prudently, a more boring version of high yield may be just the recipe for weathering an unpredictable market.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams, and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: KION: Victory Lap And 1H25 Update, Nearly Triple-Digit Returns
data.description:
Oh, KION (OTCPK:KIGRY). A company that has seen more ups and downs in a short time has to be looked for carefully. I have been positive on KION for well over 2 years at this point, since the company "dipped" significantly as a result of challenges related to business model/operations. I saw the undervaluation in the company, and invested step-by-step, but heavily, over the course of those 1-2 years, with most of my position being bought towards late 2024 and early 2025. That means that my return here is better than the returns of over 46%+ RoR since my last article in May of 2025, found here.

My returns are instead closer to the ones here, of close to 100% in less than a year. That positive article can be found here, and the returns can be seen below.

So, this is a bit of a victory lap, for lack of a better term. I've done a few of these so far this year - but it's especially gratifying when it's a company that I alone seem to be the one covering (or close to it), such as with KION. It goes to my overall assumption and the way that I invest, that value and valuation is what matter. What you pay for what you get, and that you recognize both the states of under- and overvaluation and act accordingly. The market is in constant flux, and a company can go from under- to overvalued in less than half a year - or it can take, as we see with KION, several years.

The most important thing that I have found is to be patient and never lose conviction simply because the company "seems to be going nowhere". Many readers surely have asked themselves, "When is this guy going to see that this company isn't going anywhere!"

Well, until the fundamentals or the prospects for the company change- verifiably, not based on fear - I will not do so. Over the past 3-4 years, I can count on one hand the times when I have realized a loss, and it's less than 3 - usually when I went against one of my cardinal rules for investing.

So while my approach certainly does not suit everyone - especially those requiring steady, continuous income - I'm very confident in my approach, and it suits me well.

So what do we do with KION after a triple-digit RoR?

That's what this article will be about.

KION - Plans after a 100% RoR

First off, let's look at what has caused the company's recent move upward. To assist us with this, we have the company's 2Q25 results, which were released about 2 weeks ago at the time of writing this article. The first indications for what makes this such a successful period can be found here.

Top-line saw a 30%+ increase, despite a revenue decline YoY, and EBITD decline of about 14% as well as a margin decline. FCF was down as well, but the EPS is increasing - quarterly €0.72/share, with an increase YoY of over 35%. This was in line with my own expectations overall, a slight miss on revenue and EBIT, but in line with EPS.

What was surprising was record order levels in SCS - Business solutions as a sub-segment really saw a nice upside here, and NWC releases prevented FCF from going "too far down".

It's my view that the market sending the share prices up was as a result of confirmed stability and growth on the part of KION, despite some of the overall negative trends across some segments. ITS, one of the company's core business segments, saw the most EBIT and margin impacts, and this was due to lower volumes. But as before, usually when one is down, the other is up. So too this time, with improvement in SCS/Business solutions orders. Profitability was key here, and this is what many investors have been waiting for.

And while ITS may look bad on the surface, the order intake improvement was actually visible in both segments - especially on the service side. Remember that KION is a 50/50 play on product and service sales, and that service sales revenue and EBIT is one of the major generators of recurring revenues. The main reason the company saw such poor EBIT, if you dig down into the numbers, is the company's lower cost absorption in ITS.

This is, as I see it, a momentary factor.

Net income was up as well, confirming the overall and longer-term improvement for the company, and while the company's leverage saw a significant increase from 0.5x to 1.3x, most of that was from procurement leases and STR financing - so on a net financial debt (as opposed to industrial), the company remains unchanged.

In my previous article, I briefly touched upon the subject of tariffs and SCM disruptions as a risk. One of the reasons I believe KION is up significantly is the lack of meaningful disruptions in the company's supply chains. The 1H25 is a good start to the 2025 period, in line with expectations by the company, and ahead of my own expectations, despite significant geopolitical uncertainty still being part of things here.

The company has already expanded its production capacity, research, as well as manufacturing, as well as sales and service to reflect this ongoing macro, especially in the APAC and NA region, to counter the expected scenarios that might happen. As of 1H25, the company is still expecting the growth and earnings that I detailed in my last article,

One of the main factors for further potential improvement is the company's efficiency program. The company is not yet in line with its targets for EBIT margin or other KPI's, and the target is 10%+ in margin for both of the company's operating segments - quite some ways to go in some of them. It's this that I base my long-term fair value for KION on, which I believe to be well above €75/share, given that I have a €68/share PT as of my last article. The company's native ticker is now trading at €59/share, which is still below that target.

One of the primary questions I get when looking at things like this is when I decide to "take profits" in an investment with this sort of return. Despite the fact that KION hasn't yet reached my target of above €68, let alone my fair value target, is it wrong to take a profit here?

My logic for this is relatively simple - it's never wrong to take profit, especially with a high profit as the one we're seeing here. My own adjusted RoR, inclusive of dividends and balanced for cost basis, is just north of 87%. That is a non-trivial rate of return, even if the company has more upside. My decision/s on when to take profit are dictated by the remaining upside, how conservative it is, and how volatile the company has been. I can only say that typically, I am not all that short-term oriented.

I will say, however, that several times when I have taken profit before reaching my fair-value target, I have not had cause to regret it, as the company has dropped back down.

So how do things look here?

Valuation for KION - Upside remains, I may take profit, but I don't lower (or increase) my PT.

My stance is that the company has "done what I expected it to do". All of these factors are things I forecasted in one or several of my articles. The positive trajectory for the company's earnings has been a core part of my thesis for the company for several years at this point.

That's where my fair value target of close to €95/share comes from - a 20-22x P/E on a forward basis. And on this basis, we can still see a 15% annualized or above for KION as of August 2025. For that reason, I am not lowering my PT, I am not lowering my rating, and I am, for the time being, staying in this investment.

Given, however, that KION is now over 2.5% of my total portfolio, there will come a point either in the near or distant future when I will sell part or the entirety of that position, harvest the gains, as it were. This could be due to a quick surge, or due to a longer-term upside trend. Most analysts underestimated KION. I did not. You can see the valuation target history for the last 12 months here. The best thing that can be said for them is that they never wavered in their "Buy" recommendations or overweights.

It goes to show you the value of long-term investment goals and applying the sort of logic that I do to my investments. KION might have taken a long time to "ripen", and it's still not fully valued above my PT, but it's getting there.

For the time being, based on forecasts and results, I'm happy to keep my targets and assumptions on KION, and I won't rotate. But I will tell you that going forward, I may end up rotating before my stated PT of €68/share, to realize some of those profits. The reason that I invest in the stock market is to beat comparable indices and increase my wealth. I usually tend to look at that sell button after 100%+ RoR, depending on the specific thesis.

KION has peers to invest in, if you're interested, but many of these have seen increases as well. I invest in Knorr Bremse AG (OTCPK:KNRRY), but that one has appreciated a bit too much as well. I would avoid Toyota Industries, native ticker 6201 at this time, but might look at Jungheinrich (OTCPK:JGHHY) in the future - but in the end, I continue to view KION as the best "deal" here.

Risks to my continued positive thesis are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

KION could, given its volatility, see a short-term downside once again. I view this as relatively unlikely, but it could happen if the 2025E results turn out worse than expected. However, in the last year, I believe the company has been on a bit of a winning streak, and increase in service revenue and earnings, and results improvement, despite geopolitical macro spells out a positive potential. For this reason, I am positive despite the volatility risks here.

My PT remains and comes to $19.85/share for the ADR KIGRY.

Thesis

  • KION Group remains an attractive capital goods play with an emphasis on intra-logistics solutions, automation, and warehouse technologies—things like forklifts, to put it simply. The upside for the remainder of 2025E is very much there, even with the company slightly above €55/share.

  • The company is undervalued going into the second half of 2025, and I forecast not only an upside over the coming 3 years but also continue to expect an increase in the company's dividend over time, which has now also been confirmed going into 2025, and the forecast seems clear for further improvement in 2026–2027E if earnings improve.

  • KION is a "BUY" with a price target of €68/share, updated for Q2'25 and going into 2025-2027E. This PT remains from the previous level. I may rotate and take profit if the company appreciates further, despite not having hit my long-term PT or fair value target of close to €95/share.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (Italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.

  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.

  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.

  • This company is currently cheap.

  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 2 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 5/5 "stars" here, which is more than enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of €68/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of August 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MEGI: Outlook Depends On Outlook Of Interest Rates
data.description:
Overview

NYLI CBRE Global Infrastructure Megatrends Term (NYSE:MEGI) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide an attractive total return from its portfolio of assets that put an emphasis on decarbonization, digital transformation, and modernization of infrastructure. I really like this focus because it provides a way for investors to invest in businesses that have a meaningful impact on the quality of our lives. When I previously covered MEGI, I issued a hold rating due to its questionable dividend coverage at the time. However, MEGI has released an updated annual report that prompted me to revisit the fund and reassess its performance and outlook for the remainder of the year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, MEGI saw its share price increase by about 8.2%. The fund was capable of riding the positive market momentum higher over the last few months. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps to 20.6% over the same period. One of the primary appeals of MEGI is the large starting dividend yield of about 10.3%. This double-digit yield makes it an enticing choice for investors that want to create a sizeable stream of dividend income over time.

The fund has demonstrated a vulnerability to the higher interest rate environment since its inception back in 2021. This sensitivity to rates can be attributed to the fund's use of leverage and exposure to businesses that use debt as a way to fuel operational growth. However, the market seems to anticipate the possibility of rate cuts happening before year end, which can serve as a growth catalyst for MEGI. Therefore, MEGI could certainly see its share price increase over the next twelve months. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that MEGI implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, MEGI has total managed assets of $1.1B that is allocated across a diverse range of assets. The fund utilizes a strategic blend between common equities, preferred stock, and convertible securities. When market conditions are favorable, this blend can lead to a mix of both capital appreciation and income generation over time. As the fund's name implies, MEGI has a limited term with a targeted liquidation around December of 2033. The fund also provides some global exposure, which increases the diversity and reduces the reliance on US markets to fuel growth. The breakdown by region is as follows:

  • Americas: 63%
  • Europe: 21%
  • Asia Pacific: 15%

Sticking with diversity, we can see below that MEGI maintains an efficient level of diversity across sectors. However, Utilities account for the largest bulk of assets at 55%. This is followed by exposure to Transportation and Communication investments, accounting for 16% and 15% of assets respectively. The fund allocates its assets to businesses that offer essential services, such as energy infrastructure, midstream businesses, and transportation businesses like airports and railroads.

Looking at the top holdings of the fund, we can see many large-cap recognizable names as part of the fund. For instance Norfolk Southern Corporation (NSC) is the largest holding at 4.2% of assets. This is followed by exposure to other large-cap companies such as Enel SpA (OTCPK:ESOCF), NetLink NBN (OTCPK:NETLF), Crown Castle (CCI), and Atlas Arteria (OTCPK:MAQAF). These top holdings make up approximately a third of total assets within MEGI, so investors should anticipate these to have the greatest impact on the daily price change and total return potential.

Despite the diversity across holdings, investors should limit their expectations around MEGI's ability to provide capital appreciation similar to its underlying holdings. Since MEGI prioritizes income for shareholders, a lot of the upside is exchanged for the higher income potential. Just as an example, we can see that MEGI's share price lagged behind when compared to the growth of its top three positions.

Valuation & Risk Profile

At the time of my last coverage, MEGI traded at a discount to NAV of about 11%. Following the recent market rally, MEGI now trades at a smaller discount to NAV of approximately 7.6%. As we can see below, MEGI trades at a deeper discounted valuation in comparison to its peer group, which now trades at a premium to NAV of 6.4%. I believe the discount can be attributed to the negative impact of the leverage being used by the fund. The discounted valuation can be an indication that it may be an appropriate time to start accumulating shares.

In an effort to get better clarity into the fund's performance, I reviewed the latest annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.42 per share, while net realized gains landed at $1.34 per share. Looking back on prior years, net investment income levels have been a bit inconsistent, but this may be due to the lower operating spread between the cost of MEGI's leverage and the yield collected from its securities. Since net investment income levels aren't strong enough to support distributions on its own, the fund is reliant on net realized gains to fuel growth.

The issue is that net realized gains have remained inconsistent since the fund's inception, which has made it difficult for NAV growth to occur. MEGI relies on positive market conditions so that it can sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. So as you can imagine, it's difficult for a fund to capture positive realized gains when market indices are declining. This is why the fund experienced net realized losses of $4.09 per share in 2023, which is when markets declined in response to aggressive interest rate hikes. The fund still hasn't fully recovered from its prior losses, which is why the fund still trades at a large discount to NAV. Just for reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2022: $20.70 per share
  • 2023: $16.09 per share
  • 2024: $14.96 per share
  • 2025: $15.22 per share

As part of the fund's operating strategy, MEGI utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify returns. The use of leverage currently sits around 24.3% of assets, which is equivalent to approximately $300M. However, the use of leverage can be difficult to efficiently implement in this higher interest rate environment. A higher interest rate directly translates to a higher cost of debt, which shrinks the operating spread of the fund. Investors should also consider the risk that leverage can amplify losses if allocated towards investments that do not perform as anticipated.

MEGI had the unfortunate timing of launching right before the Fed started to aggressively increase interest rates. For reference, we can see the graph below which demonstrates the inverse relationship between MEGI and the federal funds rate. The Fed aggressively increased interest rates throughout 2022 and 2023 as a way to combat record high inflationary data. The higher cost of debt has continued to keep MEGI's share price suppressed.

The market seems to be anticipating interest rate cuts to happen before year end, but I believe there's a chance that interest rate cuts may be delayed. For instance, the Fed has continued to leave rates unchanged as they monitor macroeconomic data like the unemployment rate, inflation, and shifts in consumer spending habits. Additionally, the full impact of President Trump's tariffs are still to be determined, so I believe there's a chance that the Fed will continue to delay rate cuts as a way to prevent intervening prematurely. As long as interest rates remain elevated, there's a risk that MEGI's returns continue to be suppressed.

Additionally, MEGI's complex structure may not be the best approach for investors that want to prioritize total returns. Since the majority of MEGI's assets are allocated towards Utilities, I believe that the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLU) is a solid alternative choice for long-term investors. To demonstrate, I pulled in a total return comparison below, which demonstrates that XLU's more traditional ETF structure has led to a higher total return of 44% since MEGI's inception. So despite MEGI offering a higher starting dividend yield, a high yield doesn't necessarily equate to a higher return.

Questionable Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.125 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 10.5%. If we multiply this monthly distribution by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $1.50 per share. Referring back to the same annual report for 2025, we can see that MEGI's annual earnings amounts are wildly inconsistent. For reference, here are the earnings since inception:

  • 2022: $1.24 per share
  • 2023: ($3.31) per share
  • 2024: $0.34 per share
  • 2025: $1.76 per share.

As we can see, MEGI was only able to out earn its distribution amount once since its inception. Although the lower earnings can simply be attributed to the highest interest rates, this brings the overall dividend sustainability into question. Management decided to raise the distributions back in mid 2023, which could signal their confidence of being able to out earn the distributions in the future. Therefore, I remain cautious and will be monitoring the fund's earnings as frequently as possible.

Lastly, it's worth noting that the distributions are typically funded by a combination of net investment income and net realized gains, which are usually classified as ordinary dividends. Ordinary dividends have the highest tax consequences for investors, which means that investors should anticipate a higher tax burden at the end of the year if held in a regular taxable brokerage account. To offset these concerns, a position in MEGI may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on MEGI, despite the better performance over the last operating period. Earnings have slightly increased but this may be attributed to the market rally experienced in recent months, rather than actual fundamentals of the fund. I anticipate that there's a chance that MEGI's share price will remain suppressed as long as interest rates are elevated. The recent performance has improved the dividend coverage, but the prior track record leaves me cautious of the overall sustainability of payouts in a scenario where interest rate cuts are delayed. While I do think that future interest rate cuts can serve as a catalyst, I would like to see some fundamental improvements before initiating a position.

data.title: Morgan Stanley Preferred Stock To Benefit From Fed Funds Rate Reduction
data.description:
The purpose of this brief article is to find a balanced way to benefit from a potential Fed Funds rate reduction: Morgan Stanley 4.250% Depositary Shares Non-Cumulative Preferred Stock, Series O (NYSE:MS.PR.O) is the opportunity.

Company Overview

Morgan Stanley (NYSE:MS) ranks 35th in the S&P 500 with about 0.41% weight. The market capitalization is $235 billion, with a dividend forward yield of 2.71%.

MS outperforms (SPY) for the last 10 years with total returns of 472% vs SPY's 228%.

MS has $479.19 billion in total debt. The debt-to-equity ratio is 4.08, indicating significant leverage, which is typical for a large investment bank.

Moody's assigns an A1 rating to the Senior Unsecured debt and a Baa3 rating to the preferred stock.

S&P's rating is A- for the senior debt and BBB- for the preferred stock.

Fitch rates the senior debt A+ and the preferred stock BBB.

MS debt trades OTC and can be monitored. The longest duration has a YTM of approximately 5.4%.

The preferred stock

Morgan Stanley 4.250% Depositary Shares Non-Cumulative Preferred Stock, Series O (MS.PR.O) pays an annual fixed dividend of $1.06, which amounts to 6.00% Current Yield at the last price of $17.70. The dividend is tax-qualified. In current market conditions, we seek higher yields for our income portfolio or higher credit ratings. We are looking at MS.PR.O from a different perspective. The market expects the Fed to cut rates. A lot of investment-grade securities with low nominal dividends have begun to climb. iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF) has been slowly but steadily rebounding since its year lows. On the other hand, a lot of its holdings have been lagging. We are looking for holdings that are investment grade and trade near the year lows. As the market gets more bullish on Treasuries, we expect such securities to be repriced higher. Please see some models comparing PFF and MS.PR.O :

PFF has picked up momentum, while MS.PR.O hovers over the lows.

MS.PR.K's fixed annual dividend is significantly higher, so with the recent move upside, the CY spread between MS.PR.O and MS.PR.K has widened. When the Current Yield is the same, we prefer the lower price, so there is more room for capital gains. There is also a pair trade opportunity: Short MS.PR.K, Buy MS.PR.O. until their CY spread is 40 basis points - their historical spread.

If MS.PR.K's price remains the same and the spread narrows to historical levels when MS.PR.O trades at $19.00, the price return will be 3.8% if hedged and 7.7% if naked long.

The market is pricing 3 rate cuts this year and 2 more in 2026:

This is not a tremendous opportunity in terms of yield, as the 6% dividend is fairly priced on the yield curve. Historically, fixed-income securities prices, especially investment-grade ones, have increased during rate cut cycles. We believe MS.PR.O has a reasonable 10% upside, coupled with an investment-grade tax-qualified income. At the start of the last rate cycle, MS.PR.O increased around 15%. The reason we think the risk-reward ratio is in our favour is that the security price lags market conditions both in the upside and in the downside. With an average daily volume of MS.PR.O of more than 100,000 shares, there is no shortage of liquidity both to enter at a reasonable price and exit swiftly once the environment for this investment is not favourable.

Conclusion

We are rating the security a HOLD for an income portfolio. It is a BUY if you want to trade the Fed Rate Cut scenario with a limited downside and a reaction time window. As the yield curve reprices itself, there will be a lot of opportunities in the fixed-income world. The laggards, as we say, are a great way to participate in the fixed income rally, especially when the opportunities are fundamentally sound. We will be closely monitoring for Strong Buys during the potential rate cut cycle and sending alerts to our premium members before posting an article here.

data.title: BP: Income, Growth And Cheap
data.description:
British oil and gas enterprise BP (NYSE:BP) reported better-than-expected results for Q2 last week that exceeded Wall Street estimates on both the bottom and the top line, and the company benefited from strong earnings in its core production segment. BP continues to return a lot of cash to investors as well, through both dividends and stock buybacks, and the energy firm is making an aggressive push into a number of upstream projects meant to boost BP's production growth and upstream-related earnings.

BP has seen a strong share price recovery since the market bottomed out in April, but the energy enterprise still makes a very solid value proposition, in my opinion: BP is trading at a very attractive earnings multiplier relative to U.S.-based oil and gas companies and the company has a material valuation advantage if it can expand its upstream projects.

Previous rating

I recommended shares of BP in my last work on the British energy company in May -- Takeover Speculation, Low Valuation, Capital Returns -- because I liked BP’s strong core segment profitability as well as the company’s focus on capital returns, especially with regard to stock buybacks. Further, BP is pivoting its business which is set to lead to a re-routing of CapEx spending from the renewables business to oil and gas production. I believe the valuation discount to U.S. energy companies is not deserved and I continue to recommend BP as a strong buy.

BPS beat estimates for Q2

BP reported higher-than-expected earnings and revenue for the second-quarter: the company reported adjusted EPS of $0.90, beating the average prediction by $0.24. Revenues were reported at $46.6B, which was $6.2B better than what analysts were expecting, in part due to higher production volumes vs. Q1'25.

BPA benefited from sequential production growth in its operating portfolio and was able to mitigate falling prices. BP generated $2.4B in total earnings in the second-quarter, showing a sequential rebound of 70% amid higher production, but lower realized prices for petroleum products. In the June quarter, the British energy platform realized an average price per barrel of petroleum -- in its oil production & operations segment -- of $59.74 per barrel which implies a Y/Y decline of 18%. However, BP had a total upstream production volume of 2,300 MBOED, showing 2.7% Q/Q growth.

In the oil production and operations segment, BP generated $2.26B in earnings -- shown as BP's underlying RC profit before interest and tax -- which showed a 27% year-over-year decline rate, mainly due to lower price realizations. Despite the decline in earnings, oil production and operations is by far the most profitable segment for BP... a segment that the company has said it will double down in a bid to boost its production growth.

BP earlier this year announced a major strategy shift and said that it would backtrack from pursuing its renewable energy (net zero) goals and instead pivot to its core fossil fuels business. This pivot implies $10B in annual investments (an increase of ~20%) between FY 2025 and FY 2027 as the company seeks to ramp up 10 major upstream projects, ranging from offshore investments in the Gulf of America to the BPX Energy venture operating in the Permian Basin and new development projects such as the Shah Deniz gas field in Azerbaijan.

BP is making an aggressive push into the upstream segment and earlier this year said that it plans to grow its global upstream volumes by 2.3-2.5 million barrels of oil equivalent per day by FY 2030, with U.S. operations set to be responsible for 1 million barrels of oil equivalent per day. If BP succeeds in growing its production while maintaining core business profitability, I see potential for the energy company to narrow the valuation gap between BP and its U.S. energy rivals.

Expect strong capital returns going forward...

BP announced a new $750M stock buyback for the second-quarter, to be executed before the company opens its books for Q3'25. The energy enterprise also raised its dividend by 4%. In the last five years, BP spent an average of $1.5B quarterly on stock buybacks, leading to a significant (17.25%) reduction in the company's outstanding shares. BP's push into oil and gas projects could sustain a high rate of stock buybacks going forward, especially in an environment of rising prices for petroleum.

BP has a valuation advantage compared to U.S. energy rivals

BP trades at a significant valuation discount to U.S.-based energy companies such as ExxonMobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX) and ConocoPhillips (COP). U.S. energy companies, especially those with sizable operations in the upstream business -- ExxonMobil and Chevron -- are trading at high valuation multipliers because investors expect stronger production and EPS growth relative to their European rivals.

This is chiefly due to the fact that companies like ExxonMobil and Chevron have made acquisitions in major shale oil and gas areas in the U.S., especially in the Permian Basin, to boost their production growth prospects in recent years. Currently, BP is expected to grow its earnings at an average annual rate of 9.23% in the future which is already about on the same level as ExxonMobil's expected earnings growth. However, with BP pivoting towards upstream projects, the company has the potential to accelerate both its production and EPS growth.

Shares of BP are currently valued at a price-to-earnings ratio, on a FY 2026 basis, of 11.6X compared to an industry group average P/E ratio of 14.0X. If BP were to revalue to the industry group average on improving production growth and higher core segment profitability, I could see a fair value for BP's shares in the neighborhood of $42/share... implying about 21% upside revaluation potential.

Risks with BP

BP announced earlier this year that the energy company will pivot away from its renewable energy business and focus on upstream projects in order to grow its production volumes. These projects should make positive earnings and free cash flow contributions for BP which may be a catalyst for an upside share re-pricing. However, if BP were to fail to grow its production materially and fall short of its goals, shares of the British energy enterprise could languish and BP may not succeed in narrowing the valuation gap with its U.S. energy rivals.

Final thoughts

BP is a an attractive European energy company for U.S. investors that want to diversify away from U.S.-based energy companies and potentially benefit from BP's strategic repositioning in its core oil and gas business. BP has strong core segment profitability and despite lower price realizations in its production business, the energy firm remained widely profitable in Q2'25. BP currently provides a yield of 5.7% and recently raised its dividend by 4%, while guiding for more stock buybacks. I like the relative valuation advantage of BP relative to U.S. energy companies and believe investors have potentially an arbitrage opportunity here... if BP can execute on its upstream-focused business strategy in the years ahead.

data.title: Eldorado Gold: Strong Q2 Performance Driven By Record Gold Prices, Skouries Development On Track (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

The gold mining industry is experiencing its strongest fundamental environment in decades. Gold prices have risen 40% year-over-year (Y/Y), at times surpassing the $3,500 per ounce (p/oz) level in the second quarter of 2025. This was driven by a combination of inflationary pressures, geopolitical tensions, and a strong buying rally by central banks. Meanwhile, precious metal miners, including the GDX Gold Mining ETF, have generated returns of over 70% in 2025.

This represents a fundamental shift for the gold market in recent times. Unlike previous bull runs for the metal, driven primarily by increased demand stemming from the crisis, the current cycle reflects sustained institutional positioning amid uncertainty over global monetary and trade policy. The World Gold Council reported a record Q2 2025 demand of $132B (+45% Y/Y), driven by ETF inflows, which accelerated at their fastest pace since 2020 in the first half of the year, and persistent central bank buying, which added another round 166 tonnes to its reserves.

For mid-tier producers like Eldorado Gold (NYSE:EGO), this is music to their ears. With average total operating costs hovering around $1,500 p/oz, compared to gold prices near $3,300, profit margins have expanded to historic levels, positively impacting the cash flow needed to continue funding important projects and return capital to shareholders.

Q2 2025 operating results

Diving into the numbers for this past quarter, Eldorado’s Q2 2025 production of 133,769 ounces reported its highest Q2 performance since 2020. The company’s performance was also significantly ahead of expectations due to a drawdown at Kisladag and improved production at Lamaque following increased processing of the second bulk sample from the Ormaque pit, partially offset by a lower concentrate grade per tonne which fell to 6.62 g/t from 6.95 g/t in Q2 2024 at the Canadian operation.

The increase in production was also due to seasonal dynamics, with the first quarter generally affected by winter conditions at Kisladag and their impact on longer leaching cycles. This was reflected in a drop of more than 25% ounces produced during Q1 2025, the company's lowest production in nearly two years. Despite this, and following a solid performance in Q2, company management decided to maintain fairly conservative guidance for the full year, ranging from 460,000 to 500,000 ounces, representing a 4-12% decrease compared to 2024.

Looking at the performance of each project, we see diverging trends worth highlighting. Kisladag delivered strong operating performance with 46,058 ounces (+18% Y/Y) last quarter, benefiting from accelerating inventory drawdown, while the mine operation celebrated a significant milestone of 4Moz, taking Eldorado’s cumulative gold production in Turkey to over 5Moz. On the other hand, Lamaque (again) emerged as the most productive operation of the quarter with 50,640 ounces (+6.8% Y/Y) and lower total costs of $1,231 p/oz (-11% Y/Y), demonstrating strong operating efficiency driven by improved gold concentration grades per tonne and a better than expected bulk sample from the Ormaque deposit.

Olympias showed considerable improvement, with a 35% increase in gold production and a 30% decrease in total costs compared to the previous quarter, consolidating a recovery following the resolution of its labor disputes. Meanwhile, Efemcukuru was the weakest point in the production chain, with a drop to 21,093 ounces (-6% Y/Y), attributed to a lower gold concentration per tonne, which fell from 5.92 grams in Q2 2024 to 5.75 grams.

Finally, at the consolidated level, costs continued to show persistent upward pressure, with cash costs increasing to $1,064 per ounce (+13% Y/Y) compared to $940 a year ago, while AISCs followed a similar trajectory, rising 14% from $1,331 p/oz to $1,520 p/oz over the same period. However, this did not prevent margins from resuming their upward trend during the last quarter, driven by the sharp rise in gold prices.

Financial results

Financially, results were mixed, with adjusted earnings per share of $0.44 below the consensus of $0.46, although revenue of $452M (+52% Y/Y) easily beat the analysts’ consensus of $413M. Meanwhile, the company’s free cash flow generation continues to improve. Last quarter free cash flow (excluding the Skouries investment) of $61.5M almost doubled the $34M mark reported in Q1 2025 due to a solid operating cash flow of $158M (+41% Y/Y) and a great ability to continue capitalizing on the gold price momentum. As Skouries gets underway next year, we expect this FCF trend to consolidate, as the company will be able to free up a quantity of resources previously allocated to the development of the project, whether for share buybacks or to continue expanding through new acquisitions.

In fact, this cash flow allowed the company to carry out share repurchases during Q2 2025 for approximately $45M and strengthen its balance sheet, with record total liquidity above $1,000M, giving it greater financial flexibility.

Skouries: The Catalyst for Transformation

Total capital expenditures in the second quarter of 2025 totalized around $241M, with the majority of that being allocated to the Skouries project. This copper-gold project represents Eldorado’s largest strategic initiative, with construction now 70% complete and initial production expected in the first quarter of 2026 and commercial production beginning mid-next year. The project received a substantial investment of $117 million in the Q2 2025 to an accumulated of $706M since restart of construction.

The Skouries project's anticipated copper production, in addition to gold, represents a significant advantage given this material's role in the energy transition, which gives Eldorado an attractive advantage in the coming years. If the project schedule progresses as planned, the company is positioned for significant production growth by 2027. Estimates for Skouries include proven and probable reserves of 3.7Moz of gold and 1.7 billion pounds of copper, with expected annual production of roughly 140,000 ounces of gold and 67 million pounds of copper over a 20-year mine life, which will be added to Eldorado's portfolio in the coming years.

Valuation and the path toward $2.5B in gold sales

Eldorado's stock has performed exceptionally well this year, rising more than 50% on the back of gold's momentum and Skouries' expectations. While the company lagged its industry peers somewhat in the early months of 2025, the longer-term picture tells a different story.

In terms of valuation, we see that EGO remains well below the industry average according to various multiple criteria, still indicating room for further growth despite the strong appreciation we've seen so far this year.

Following a sum-of-parts approach, and valuing current operations at the industry average of 8.8 times a normalized EBITDA of $450M, we obtain an EV of $4B. Adding Skouries' NPV estimates of approximately $1.6B (with a gold price of $1,800 and copper at $3.8), the total enterprise value would amount to $5.6B or ~$27 per share, compared to a current EV of around $4.64B or ~$22 per share, representing a potential upside of 23% and assuming fairly conservative criteria when valuing the Skouries project. Similarly, with a P/E of 14 and a trailing 12-month EPS of $2, we obtain a value per share of approximately $28, indicating a potential undervaluation of 24%, also very much in line with the previous criteria.

Furthermore, the cash flow projections from increased production resulting from the commissioning of Skouries raise the possibility that the company could soon reach $2.4 billion in gold sales revenue. The usual wear and tear at older mines would be offset in the coming years by the ramp-up of Skouries, making it the company's flagship project by 2030.

With the exception of Olympias, which recently experienced operational difficulties, we believe that following the recent plant expansion and operational improvements, production will improve to 85,000 ounces per year. Meanwhile, the remaining projects will see a gradual decline in performance over the coming years, having passed their peak production. In the case of Lamaque, the model assumes a production plateau between 180,000 and 185,000 ounces, with a brief boost from the development of Ormaque during 2026, before beginning a gradual decline to 170,000 ounces.

These projections take into account gold prices that are quite conservative in relation to some analysts' forecasts, as well as production aligned with the management goals set for the next couple of years. Assuming that the entire production output is placed on the market, with just over 660,000 ounces of gold and an estimated price of $3,800, Eldorado's revenues would exceed $2.5B by 2029. Meanwhile, including revenues from copper, revenues would reach almost $3B in 2030. This does not include other types of revenues, such as those from silver and zinc production, which have a very low weight in the company's earnings mix.

Adjusting these flows to determine the cash flow to the firm (FCFF) and discounting them at a WACC of 7% would yield an approximate value of $31 for EGO shares, indicating a potential undervaluation of 38% compared to current levels. If we also consider a possible decrease in the number of shares in the coming years as a result of the company's buybacks, this price could be even higher, while the discount rate criterion is also somewhat conservative given that the long-term WACC for the mining industry typically ranges between 5% and 6%.

On the other hand, performing a sensitivity analysis, we find that the risk-reward ratio remains attractive at current levels. Even under scenarios involving a decline in production, coupled with a contraction in gold prices, we note that the floor would be ~5% below current levels, at $21.50 per share, while the upside potential is considerable in the remaining scenarios.

Risks

As mentioned in a previous article, Eldorado operates through multiple jurisdictions with different risk profiles, which is one of the company's biggest risk factors. The resolution of labor disputes at Olympias and strong local support for the Skouries project helped stabilize operations in Greece. However, in Turkey, where two major EGO projects operate, regulatory and foreign exchange challenges continue to unfold. I strongly believe this deserves the utmost attention from investors given the rising cost inflation across all of EGO's operations in that country, which has significantly deteriorated its cost position relative to the industry.

On the opposite, Canadian operations, with a lower jurisdictional risk profile, coupled with the strategic value of assets like Skouries, present an opportunity for significant differentiation in the coming years and to significantly improve the company's overall cost structure. By spreading fixed costs across larger production volumes and benefiting from high-demand byproducts such as copper, Skouries should significantly reduce costs, addressing the current biggest problem and making it more competitive. However, I believe we won't see this until 2027, when the project is fully operational.

Conclusion

Eldorado represents an attractive investment opportunity at current levels. The completion and commissioning of Skouries is a game changer for the company to transform into a producer of over 600k ounces with significant free cash flow generation capacity. This, coupled with favorable fundamentals in the gold market positions the company for sustained growth in coming years. For these reasons I upgrade my target price estimate to a range between $28 and $31 considering conservative assumptions.

The performance data shown represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Returns for periods shorter than one year are cumulative, and results for other share classes will vary. Shares will fluctuate in value and, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance cited. Performance figures assume that all distributions are reinvested. For the most recent month-end performance, visit jhinvestments.com.

Market review and outlook

The world equity markets rebounded from initial weakness to register strong gains in the second quarter. Stocks fell sharply in early April in response to U.S. tariff announcements on concerns that Washington’s new approach to trade would lead to slower economic growth. Within a week, however, the U.S. administration reversed course and declared a 90-day pause on tariffs. The move fueled hopes that the initial plan was largely a negotiating tactic, providing investors with confidence that trade policy would ultimately be less restrictive than first thought. In addition, data related to global growth, inflation, and corporate earnings exhibited stability despite the shifting policy backdrop.

Equities recovered all of their earlier losses in the ensuing rally, propelling the major world indexes to all-time highs by the end of June. Growth stocks, lower-quality companies, and the mega-cap U.S. technology sector led the way higher, while the value style and defensive investments generally underperformed. This marked a pronounced reversal from the trends in place during the first quarter.

Bonds also delivered positive total returns. After experiencing volatility early in the quarter, the fixed-income markets recovered on the strength of low inflation, moderate growth, and expectations that central banks would continue to ease monetary policy. Credit-oriented market segments performed well in this environment and generally outpaced government debt.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation detracted from results, as our diversified approach was out of step with the aggressive rally. The real assets portfolio was the primary detractor due to negative returns for energy stocks and U.S. real estate investment trusts. An overweight position in defensive equities—while positive in absolute terms — also weighed on relative performance at a time of heightened investor risk appetites. On the positive side, the fund benefited from having an overweight in stocks relative to bonds. Allocation in the fixed-income portfolio was a small contributor, primarily as a result of an overweight in emerging-market bonds.

Underlying manager performance detracted somewhat, with nearly all of the shortfall occurring in the equity portfolio.

Portfolio changes

The first half of the year was characterized by dramatic swings in the markets, both in terms of price movements and investors’ rapid shifts among specific asset classes. For our part, we don’t attempt to time the market or chase momentum in certain areas that happen to be outperforming in a given month. Instead, we remain focused on the core tenets of our investment process: fundamentals, valuations, and diversification. While we frequently make small changes to the portfolio, the moves are based on rigorous analysis and a three- to five-year outlook. In addition, we strive to keep the portfolio positioned for a broad range of outcomes instead of trying to predict the path of the economy or the direction of government policy. We believe this steady approach, rather than one that attempts to react to the ever-changing daily headlines, is the optimal way to help the fund achieve its longer-term investment objectives.

Footnotes

1 Class R4 shares were first offered on 11/7/13 and ceased operations from 6/15/16 to 4/7/17. Returns while Class R4 shares were not offered are those of Class 1 shares (first offered on 11/7/13) that have not been adjusted for class-specific expenses; otherwise, returns would vary. Class A shares were first offered on 6/21/21. Returns prior to this date are those of Class 1 shares that have not been adjusted for class-specific expenses; otherwise, returns would vary.

2 Comparison against the benchmark and Morningstar category is based on Class R6 shares.

3 It is not possible to invest in an index. Index definitions can be found within the "What you should know before Investing" section on the last page of this fact sheet.

4 “Net (what you pay)” represents the effect of a contractual fee waiver and/or expense reimbursement and is subject to change.

5 Listed holdings reflect the largest portions of the fund’s total and may change at any time. They are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Data is expressed as a percentage of net assets and excludes cash and cash equivalents.

This commentary reflects the views of the portfolio managers named and is subject to change as market and other conditions warrant. No forecasts are guaranteed. This commentary is provided for informational purposes only and is not an endorsement of any security, mutual fund, sector, or index. The subadvisor, the advisor (John Hancock Investment Management LLC), and their affiliates, employees, and clients, may hold or trade the securities mentioned in this commentary. Diversification does not guarantee a profit or eliminate the risk of a loss.
